chunk_summary: This section discusses food insecurity screening and dietary diversity counseling as critical interventions to address stunting and chronic undernutrition, emphasizing practical strategies like the Hunger Vital Sign tool, anganwadi nutrition, school meals, and nutrient-dense dietary swaps tailored to local contexts.
learning_objectives: Understand the role of food insecurity screening in addressing stunting and chronic undernutrition.
Learn how to use the Hunger Vital Sign tool for rapid food insecurity assessment.
Identify practical dietary counseling strategies to improve nutrient density and dietary diversity.
Explore the integration of anganwadi and school meal programs into nutrition interventions.
key_facts: Food insecurity is defined as the inability to consistently access sufficient, safe, and nutritious food for an active life.
The Hunger Vital Sign tool uses two questions to screen for food insecurity: worry about food running out and food not lasting.
For under-6 children, anganwadi supplementary nutrition and growth monitoring are essential interventions.
School meals provide a reliable calorie and protein baseline for school-age children.
Dietary counseling should focus on adding one stable protein source daily (e.g., egg, milk, pulses) and upgrading energy density with small amounts of oil or ghee.
ICMR-NIN dietary guidelines serve as a framework for promoting food-group diversity.
reference_quote: The two-question Hunger Vital Sign provides a rapid screen: families are at risk when worry about food running out or food not lasting is sometimes or often true.
